personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, the real Reg Cattermole appears only briefly: he is described as pale and wan after vomiting (from the Polyjuice potion Ron used), and his only line is a confused 'Hey – what's going on? What is this?' upon seeing two versions of himself. His personality is barely sketched — he is an ordinary Ministry employee, bewildered and physically weakened, not heroic or villainous.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus is entirely empty. Reg Cattermole does not appear in any provided film scene.
personality_voice.difference: The character is absent from the films entirely. No voice, no personality, no behavior is conveyed.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: Complete absence means no personality or voice is preserved. The score cannot be above the floor range.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced — the film corpus is empty.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Reg's narrative function is structural: his identity is borrowed by Ron via Polyjuice Potion, enabling the trio's infiltration of the Ministry. His physical reappearance at the end of the scene creates the comedic and tense complication of two Reg Cattermoles confronting each other and his wife. He is a plot device whose real-world existence generates the crisis that forces the trio to flee.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Absent from the film corpus. His role as the 'real Reg' creating the double-identity crisis is not shown.
narrative_role_agency.difference: His narrative function — the complication of the double — is either cut or handled differently in the film without his physical presence.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The absence of the real Reg removes his only agency: appearing and triggering the crisis. This is a complete loss of narrative role.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced — film corpus is empty.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: No internal conflict is established for Reg in the books beyond implied concern for his wife (she is on trial for being Muggle-born). His motivation is simply to find her. This is minimal but present.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: Not present in the film corpus.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: Any motivation, however thin, is absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Directly evidenced — film corpus is empty.

character_arc.book_baseline: Reg has no arc in the traditional sense — he is a flat, functional character who appears at the end of the Ministry sequence as a complication. His 'arc' is simply: absent, then present, then bewildered.
character_arc.film_portrayal: Not present in the film corpus.
character_arc.difference: Even this minimal presence is absent.
character_arc.penalty_logic: No arc can be evaluated or preserved when the character does not appear.
character_arc.evidence_status: Directly evidenced — film corpus is empty.

key_relationships.book_baseline: His key relationship is with Mary Cattermole (his wife). Her relief at seeing 'Reg' (actually Ron) and then her confusion upon seeing the real Reg establishes a genuine emotional bond — she is desperate to reunite with him and flee with their children. The real Reg's appearance complicates this dynamic.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: Not present in the film corpus.
key_relationships.difference: The real Reg's relationship with Mary cannot be conveyed if he does not appear.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Complete absence of the relationship dynamic.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced — film corpus is empty.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Reg's complexity is minimal but specific: the image of a pale, vomiting, bewildered man confronting a duplicate of himself while his wife looks between them is a small but vivid human moment. It grounds the Polyjuice sequence in real-world consequence — there is an actual person whose identity was stolen.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: Not present in the film corpus.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The humanizing detail of the real Reg — the physical cost of the Polyjuice on him, his confusion, the double-identity confrontation — is entirely absent.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: All nuance, however limited, is lost.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced — film corpus is empty.

lost_or_transferred_material: The real Reg Cattermole's physical appearance at the end of the Ministry sequence, creating the double-identity crisis
His only line of dialogue: 'Hey – what's going on? What is this?'
The visual/comedic beat of Mrs. Cattermole looking between two versions of her husband
The humanizing detail that he was physically ill (pale and wan from the Polyjuice effect on Ron)
His function as the complication that forces the trio to flee the Ministry
score_caps_applied: Cap 4: Character scores below 70 — films preserve only the broad identity (name referenced via Ron's disguise) but remove the actual character's appearance and all defining material.
Cap 5 considered: motivations and arc are absent rather than incoherent, but the result is the same — score remains low.
comparative.key_observations: Reginald Cattermole is a minor functional character in the books whose sole purpose is to provide Ron's Polyjuice identity and then appear in person to create the crisis that ends the Ministry sequence. The film corpus is entirely empty — he does not appear at all in the provided screenplay material. This means no dimension of his book characterization is preserved in the films. All scores are at or near the floor, and all confidence levels are Low due to the complete absence of film evidence. The low total score (16) reflects not a rich character being badly adapted, but a thin character being entirely absent — which is itself a faithful reflection of how little there was to adapt, tempered by the fact that his specific narrative function (the double confrontation) appears to have been cut.
